content.text:
<timestamp>Monday, Apr 27, 2026, 9:47 AM (UTC-4)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I'm getting 12 unlisted dependencies in my fallow report when running `pnpm run fallow` from the root. Why is this? Does fallow not see my workspace dependencies?
</user_query>
I need to look into how fallow handles unlisted dependencies in this codebase.
I need to run fallow to see the actual report and identify which dependencies are missing from the listing.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm fallow dead-code --format json 2>&1 | tail -100
input.description: Get full unlisted deps from json

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow dead-code --format json 2>/dev/null | tail -200
input.description: Get JSON output for full deps list

content.text: All the unlisted dependencies are coming from `apps/org-next/`, so I need to check that package.json to see what's actually declared there.

I'm checking the ignore patterns in the fallow configuration and noticing that `apps/org-next` isn't explicitly listed there, even though other app directories are being ignored—which might explain why fallow is flagging those dependencies as unlisted despite them being present in the package.json. Ah, I see the issue now — fallow running from the root is checking dependencies against the root `package.json`, not the workspace's own `package.json`. There's an untracked `.fallowrc.json` in `apps/org-next` that I should examine to understand how the workspace-level configuration is set up.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/org-next && pnpm exec fallow dead-code 2>&1 | tail -40

content.text:
I can already see what's happening. Let me confirm by checking the fallow documentation about how it handles monorepo workspaces.

 When fallow runs from `apps/org-next` where the local config sits, it correctly identifies the 12 dependencies listed there and only flags `msw` as an issue since it's in the root devDeps. But when running from the root, fallow loads the main config, discovers all 19 workspaces, and processes plugins across them—which is where the behavior diverges.

Looking at the root config's `ignorePatterns`, it excludes other workspace directories like `apps/auth/**` and `apps/builder/**` but notably leaves `apps/org-next/**` unignored, and it also filters out config files and test files. The root entry point is set to `["apps/**"]`, treating all files in app directories as entry points. The puzzle is why fallow at the root doesn't resolve those 12 dependencies against the `apps/org-next/package.json` where they're actually declared—it seems like a workspace boundary or package.json association issue where fallow is analyzing the files but not correctly linking them to the local workspace's dependencies.

The key insight is that fallow discovered 19 workspaces and shows workspace-specific plugins, yet the final report has a single `unlisted_dependencies` array rather than per-workspace results. When running from inside `apps/org-next`, fallow correctly identifies only `msw` as unlisted (since it's in the root's devDependencies), but running from root reports 12 unlisted—suggesting fallow is evaluating everything against the root's `package.json` rather than respecting workspace-level dependency declarations.

I suspect fallow needs explicit workspace configuration in the root `.fallowrc.json` to properly scope the analysis, similar to how Knip handles workspaces. Let me check the fallow documentation to confirm this behavior.

I'm checking whether fallow's workspace auto-detection gets blocked by the root config's `ignorePatterns` setting. The root config ignores most of the `packages/` and `apps/` directories, but notably leaves `apps/org-next/` unignored, so I need to trace through how that affects workspace boundary detection.

Fallow discovered 19 workspaces and 131 entry points, which seems like a lot. Looking at the ignore patterns more carefully, I see they're excluding the package directories themselves—when `packages/**` is ignored, it's ignoring all the workspace package.json files inside. But the root config also has `entry: ["apps/**"]`, which should treat anything in apps as entry points, minus the explicitly ignored subdirectories. So `apps/org-next` should be analyzed since it's not in the ignore list.

Now I'm trying to understand why there are unlisted dependencies being reported—the 12 unlisted deps might be coming from how the entry points are being resolved against the workspace boundaries. I think the root cause is that when I specify `entry: ["apps/**"]` in the root config, fallow treats all those source files as belonging to the root workspace rather than their individual workspaces, so dependency checks run against the root package.json instead of each app's own dependencies. The solution is either to remove that manual entry config and let workspace auto-detection handle it, or to run fallow per-workspace instead of from the root.

Let me dig into why fallow isn't matching the imports against the workspace's package.json.

 Looking at the root config more carefully, I see that `entry` adds to the auto-detected entry points from package.json rather than replacing them, and workspaces are automatically detected from pnpm-workspace.yaml. Now I'm examining the ignore patterns and public packages configuration to understand how the root setup filters and exposes packages.

The key distinction is that `ignorePatterns` completely excludes files from analysis, so `packages/**` and most apps are filtered out entirely, but `apps/org-next/**` remains included. When fallow analyzes `apps/org-next/src/lib/rollbar.ts` at the root level, I'm wondering whether it validates dependencies against the root package.json or the workspace's own package.json. The fact that running fallow inside `apps/org-next` shows only one unlisted dependency (msw) while the root shows twelve suggests that fallow might be treating the root as the package being analyzed rather than validating each workspace independently. I'm re-reading the documentation to understand whether the workspace structure is used primarily for resolution or for per-package dependency validation.

The docs mention "scoping output to a single package" via the `-w` flag, implying that by default all workspaces are analyzed against their respective package.jsons. But the 47 manual entry points from `entry: ["apps/**"]` might be the culprit—these could be attributed to the root workspace rather than to the individual app workspaces, causing transitive imports from those entries to be checked against the root's package.json instead. The fix would likely be removing the root-level `entry` config and letting workspace auto-detection handle entries from each package.json, or moving that configuration into per-workspace `.fallowrc.json` files.

Looking at the logs more carefully, I can see fallow is resolving source files in packages/ even though they're in ignorePatterns, which confirms packages/ is recognized as workspaces. When org-next runs with its own `.fallowrc.json`, it extends the root config but overrides `entry` to `src/**/*` (relative to that app). The question is whether fallow running from the root even loads those nested `.fallowrc.json` files—the docs suggest per-package configs are only loaded when running from within those packages or when scoping with the `-w` flag.

Let me check what `fallow list` shows to understand the actual behavior, or test removing `entry: ["apps/**"]` from the root config to see if that changes how dependencies are resolved.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --workspaces 2>&1 | tail -60
cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --help 2>&1 | head -40
cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --plugins 2>&1 | tail -80
cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow dead-code --workspace org-next 2>&1 | tail -30

content.text:
Let me check git history and understand what changed recently relative to this issue.

 So when I scope fallow to just the org-next workspace, it reports zero unlisted dependencies, but at the root level without any scope it finds 12. That's counterintuitive—I'd expect scoping to be more restrictive, not less. The docs mention that workspace flags focus the output but still analyze the full monorepo graph, so there's something about how the reporting works that's causing this difference.

I think the root cause might be the `entry` glob at the root level. When fallow registers those manual entry points from `apps/**`, it might be treating them as belonging to the root package rather than attributing them to their respective workspaces. So when checking imports from those entries, it validates against the root's package.json instead of the workspace-specific ones. But when I use `-w org-next`, fallow correctly scopes the analysis to org-next's own package.json, which actually has those dependencies listed, so no issues appear.

Let me verify this by checking the config more carefully—the detection showed 47 manual entry points from that glob pattern, which is likely where the mismatch is happening. The user's question about "workspace dependencies" is a bit ambiguous — they could mean either the dependencies listed in the local package.json or the workspace:* packages. Looking at the 12 unlisted deps, they're all third-party packages like rollbar and lucide-react, not workspace references, so they're asking about the former. Let me check if there's more context in the conversation history.

The `.fallowrc.json` file is untracked in git, so this is a new configuration being developed. I'm suspecting the manual `entry: ["apps/**"]` setting might be causing the issue, but I can't test changes in plan mode. Let me approach this differently by checking whether the `-w org-next` flag's scoping behavior might be suppressing unlisted issues.

According to the docs, scoping with `-w` filters the output but analyzes the full monorepo graph, so if there are 12 unlisted issues at root, they should still appear when scoped to org-next unless they're attributed to the root workspace itself. Let me test with `-w monorepo` to check the root workspace directly.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow dead-code --workspace @workspace/monorepo 2>&1 | tail -30

content.text: I'm noticing that the root workspace `@workspace/monorepo` isn't showing up as a valid workspace target, which suggests fallow excludes the root project from workspace targeting. I should check if the manual entries from the root config are still being attributed to root somehow by running `fallow list --entry-points`.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points 2>&1 | rg -i "rollbar|sonner|lucide|fontsource|^/" | head -40
cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points 2>&1 | rg "manual entry" | head -60

content.text:
The manual entries from the root config's `entry: ["apps/**"]` are pulling in 47 files from apps/org-next as entry points, but they're being attributed to the root workspace rather than org-next itself. This explains why running with `-w org-next` shows no issues while the root run surfaces 12 issues—the manual entries aren't scoped to the org-next workspace.

The solution is either to remove that glob pattern from the root config or move it to a workspace-specific .fallowrc.json. I'm also checking what entries org-next auto-detects from its own package.json, but it doesn't have main, bin, or exports fields, so those won't contribute any entries. However, fallow's plugins like vite and tanstack-router are still adding their own entries to the analysis.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points 2>&1 | rg "org-next" | head -80

content.text:
I have enough to diagnose this. Let me verify one final detail about how fallow attributes entries to workspaces.

 So org-next's entries are all manual from the root config, with just a couple from the msw plugin—there's no auto-detection happening because the package.json lacks the standard entry point fields like `main`, `module`, `bin`, or `exports` since it's a private app. I'm noticing something similar might be happening with the fallow auto-detection.

Looking at the plugin list for org-next (tanstack-router, vite, vitest, playwright, biome, typescript, tailwind), each should normally contribute entry points, but I'm not seeing them in the actual entry list—they might be getting deduplicated with the manual entries. The real issue is that all these entries are attributed to the root config context rather than the workspace itself, so fallow checks dependencies against the root package.json, which doesn't have lucide-react or rollbar, and flags them as unlisted. The fix would be running per-workspace analysis with auto-detection of each workspace's own package.json instead of using a top-level glob that crosses workspace boundaries.

Both root and org-next define fallow scripts, and org-next's works fine (only flagging msw as a real issue), but the root one breaks because of that entry configuration. The docs clarify that `entry` is meant for files not auto-detected by package.json or plugins—the user likely added `entry: ["apps/**"]` thinking it would scope analysis to the apps directory, but it actually registers all those files as manual entries owned by the root config. So the 12 unlisted dependencies are all actually listed in org-next's package.json, just not in the root's.

The evidence confirms this: all 12 deps are imported only from org-next files, they're all in org-next/package.json but not root, and running fallow with workspace scoping shows zero issues. The fix is to remove that entry glob from the root config since fallow's per-workspace auto-detection already handles entry discovery correctly through pnpm-workspace.yaml and each workspace's own configuration. I'm seeing that npm-workspace.yaml is what fallow uses to auto-detect workspaces, and if I remove the root-level `entry` config, fallow should correctly attribute dependencies to each workspace based on their individual package.json files and plugin detection. There's also a production mode difference — the org-next config runs in production mode (excluding devDeps) while the root run doesn't, which might explain why some devDependencies are flagged at root but not in the workspace-specific run. I have enough context now to explain the root cause and propose a fix.

input.command: cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points 2>&1 | rg -v "manual entry" | rg "org-next"
cd /Users/duhl/git/ui && pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points 2>&1 | rg "package.json" | head -20

content.text:
So org-next itself doesn't contribute any entries from its package.json since it's a private app with no exports defined. The entries showing up are actually coming from the root config's glob pattern that matches all apps, which means they're tagged as manual entries belonging to the root context, not the org-next workspace. This is why when fallow validates unlisted dependencies for those entries, it's checking against the root package.json instead.

The core issue is that org-next has 12 dependencies listed in its own package.json that aren't in root, so they fail the check. The real solution is to either run fallow per-workspace through Turbo or remove that cross-workspace glob from the root config entirely. I'm leaning toward using per-workspace fallow runs since org-next already has its own .fallowrc.json in progress with workspace-specific entries, and the package.json script is already set up. The question is whether removing the root-level entry glob would break entry detection for other apps that might not have their own plugins configured.

I have a clear diagnosis. Before drafting a plan, I want to confirm the preferred fix direction since there are two valid approaches.

## What's happening

All 12 reported imports are reachable only from `apps/org-next/**` source files, and all 12 are correctly listed in [apps/org-next/package.json](apps/org-next/package.json) (`rollbar`, `sonner`, `lucide-react`, `zod`, `@tanstack/react-form`, `@tanstack/react-query`, `tw-animate-css`, `@fontsource-variable/outfit`, `@tanstack/react-devtools`, `@tanstack/react-router-ssr-query`, `@tanstack/start-server-core`, `request-mocking-protocol`).

The root cause is the `"entry": ["apps/**"]` glob in [.fallowrc.json](.fallowrc.json):

```1:42:.fallowrc.json
{
  "ignorePatterns": [
    "apps/auth/**",
    ...
  ],
  "publicPackages": [...],
  "entry": ["apps/**"],
  ...
}
```

Per the [fallow `entry` docs](https://docs.fallow.tools/configuration/overview#entry), `entry` adds files as additional manual entry points. Because that glob is set in the root config, every file it matches in `apps/org-next/**` is registered as a manual entry point owned by the **root** config context, not by the `org-next` workspace.

You can see this directly in `fallow list --entry-points`: every `org-next` source file is tagged `(manual entry)` (only the two MSW files come from a plugin). That happens because `apps/org-next/package.json` has no `main`/`module`/`exports`/`bin` fields, so fallow's per-workspace package.json auto-detection produces zero entry points for it — leaving only your root `entry` glob to discover them.

Evidence this is the cause:
- Running fallow from inside `apps/org-next/` (using its own [.fallowrc.json](apps/org-next/.fallowrc.json) with `"entry": ["src/**/*"]`) shows only 1 unlisted (`msw`, a real issue: it's used by `tests/test-fixture.ts` but only in root `devDependencies`).
- `fallow dead-code -w org-next` from root shows 0 issues — workspace scoping correctly attributes those files to the org-next workspace's `package.json`.
- The root `package.json` does not declare `rollbar`, `sonner`, etc., so the dep check fails when those imports are attributed to root.

So no, fallow does see your workspace dependencies — but only when an entry point belongs to that workspace. A root-level `entry` glob bypasses that attribution.

## Fix options

 I'm realizing the user is asking for an explanation of why this is happening, so I should walk through the root cause and then present them with a couple of different approaches to fix it—since there are valid ways to solve this depending on what they prefer.

questions.prompt: Which fix would you like the plan to use?

options.label: Run fallow per-workspace via Turbo (root `pnpm fallow` -> `turbo run fallow`); each workspace owns its own .fallowrc.json + entries
Keep a single root `fallow` invocation; remove `entry: ["apps/**"]` from root and rely on per-workspace auto-detection / per-app .fallowrc.json
Don't change anything yet — I just wanted the explanation

content.text:
I'll go with the cleanest fix that fits the existing patterns in the repo. Here's the plan.

 Since the user skipped ahead, I'll move forward with the per-workspace approach using Turbo—it's the cleanest solution and fits the existing patterns in the repo. The org-next package already has its own config that extends the root one, so I'll design a plan around that structure.

Looking at the root config's ignorePatterns, I realize it's already excluding most apps and only running fallow for org-next anyway. So the whole root setup is actually over-engineered for what we're trying to accomplish. The simplest fix is to either stop running fallow from root entirely and just run it from inside org-next, or change the root script to target org-next specifically with pnpm filtering.

I'm leaning toward Option A — updating the root fallow script to use pnpm filtering and removing the root .fallowrc.json since it's untracked and unnecessary. But there's also Option B of setting up fallow as a proper turbo task where each workspace defines its own script, which is more general and scalable.

Either way, I need to flag that the msw unlisted dependency finding is legitimate — it's actually used in org-next's test fixtures but only declared in the root package.json, so that's a real issue to address in the plan.

input.name: fix root fallow unlisted deps
input.overview: Stop reporting 12 false-positive unlisted dependencies by removing the cross-workspace `entry` glob from the root fallow config and routing root `pnpm run fallow` through per-workspace runs (so each workspace's package.json is what fallow checks against). Also fix the one genuine finding (`msw`) it surfaces.
input.plan:
## Diagnosis recap

`fallow dead-code` from the repo root reports 12 unlisted deps that are all already declared in [apps/org-next/package.json](apps/org-next/package.json) (`rollbar`, `sonner`, `lucide-react`, `zod`, `@tanstack/react-form`, `@tanstack/react-query`, `tw-animate-css`, `@fontsource-variable/outfit`, `@tanstack/react-devtools`, `@tanstack/react-router-ssr-query`, `@tanstack/start-server-core`, `request-mocking-protocol`). The cause is in [.fallowrc.json](.fallowrc.json):

```1:42:.fallowrc.json
{
  "ignorePatterns": [
    "apps/auth/**",
    ...
    "packages/**",
    ...
  ],
  "entry": ["apps/**"],
  ...
}
```

`entry` adds manual entry points owned by the config that declared them. Because `apps/org-next/package.json` has no `main`/`module`/`exports` fields, fallow's per-workspace package.json auto-detection produces zero entries for it. The root `entry: ["apps/**"]` then fills the gap, but those entries are attributed to the root context, so fallow checks the root `package.json` for the imported deps and they aren't there. `fallow list --entry-points` confirms it: every `org-next` source file is tagged `(manual entry)`.

Two pieces of supporting evidence:
- Inside [apps/org-next/](apps/org-next/), `fallow dead-code` (using the local [.fallowrc.json](apps/org-next/.fallowrc.json)) reports only 1 unlisted: `msw` (a real bug — it's imported by [apps/org-next/tests/test-fixture.ts](apps/org-next/tests/test-fixture.ts) but only declared in root `devDependencies`).
- `fallow dead-code -w org-next` from root reports 0 issues, because `-w` re-attributes findings to the workspace whose `package.json` actually owns the deps.

Right now the root config is also gated to org-next only — every other app and all of `packages/**` are in `ignorePatterns`. So a single root run isn't earning much over a per-workspace run.

## Approach

Run fallow per workspace via the existing Turbo pipeline (matches how `lint`, `typecheck`, `test`, `e2e` already work), and remove the cross-workspace `entry` glob that misattributes ownership.

## Changes

1. Add a `fallow` task to [turbo.json](turbo.json) so Turbo can fan out per workspace:

```json
"fallow": {
  "dependsOn": ["^build"],
  "outputs": []
}
```

   `^build` is needed because some workspace `package.json` `exports` point at `dist/`; fallow's logs show the fallback path mapping working, but matching the existing `typecheck`/`test` tasks keeps caching consistent.

2. Replace the root `fallow` scripts in [package.json](package.json) so the root run delegates:

```json
"fallow": "turbo run fallow",
"fa": "turbo run fallow --"
```

3. Simplify [.fallowrc.json](.fallowrc.json) at the repo root so it stops registering cross-workspace entries:
   - Remove `"entry": ["apps/**"]` (per-workspace configs and plugin auto-detection cover this).
   - Remove all `apps/<app>/**` and `packages/**` entries from `ignorePatterns` — they only mattered to scope the root `entry` glob; with per-workspace runs, each workspace decides what it analyzes.
   - Keep `publicPackages`, `health`, and the language-level ignore globs (`**/*.gen.ts`, `**/*.config.ts`, etc.) so they're inherited via `extends` by every workspace `.fallowrc.json`.

4. Make the org-next config first-class instead of untracked:
   - Track [apps/org-next/.fallowrc.json](apps/org-next/.fallowrc.json) (currently `??` in `git status`) so the local `entry: ["src/**/*"]` and zone boundaries are versioned.
   - Add a `.gitignore` rule for `.fallow/cache.bin` and `.fallow/churn.bin` (already present in untracked dirs at repo root and `apps/org-next/`) so those incremental caches stop showing up as new files.

5. For other workspaces that should be analyzed (or explicitly skipped), add a `fallow` script in their `package.json`:
   - Apps you want analyzed: `"fallow": "fallow dead-code && fallow dupes"` plus a local `.fallowrc.json` extending the root.
   - Apps you don't want analyzed yet: omit the script — Turbo will simply skip them. (No need for the old `ignorePatterns` block at the root.)

6. Fix the genuine `msw` finding surfaced by the local org-next run. `msw` is used by [apps/org-next/tests/test-fixture.ts](apps/org-next/tests/test-fixture.ts) but only declared in root `devDependencies`. Either:
   - Add `"msw": "catalog:"` to `devDependencies` in [apps/org-next/package.json](apps/org-next/package.json), or
   - Add `"msw"` to `ignoreDependencies` in the root or org-next `.fallowrc.json` if you intentionally want it hoisted.

   Recommended: add it as a real devDep in org-next so the relationship is explicit.

7. Sanity-check by running:
   - `pnpm run fallow` from the repo root (should now fan out via Turbo and pass).
   - `pnpm --filter org-next run fallow` (should pass after step 6).
   - `pnpm exec fallow list --entry-points` from `apps/org-next/` to confirm entries are now sourced from the workspace's own config / plugins, not from a root manual glob.

## Why not just remove `entry: ["apps/**"]` and keep one root run?

That alone fixes the false positives, but it leaves you with a root config that quietly only analyzes one workspace (everything else is in `ignorePatterns`) — confusing as more apps adopt fallow. Per-workspace runs via Turbo make ownership explicit, give each app its own cache, and match the rest of the build pipeline.

todos.content: Add `fallow` task to turbo.json (dependsOn ^build, no outputs)
Update root package.json `fallow`/`fa` scripts to delegate to `turbo run fallow`
Remove `entry: ["apps/**"]` and per-workspace `ignorePatterns` from root .fallowrc.json; keep publicPackages, health, and language-level globs
Add `msw` to apps/org-next/package.json devDependencies (catalog:) to resolve the genuine unlisted finding
Run `pnpm run fallow` from root and from apps/org-next to confirm 0 unlisted dependencies
